Bill C-300 aims to modernize the legal definition of marriage in Canada to include same-sex spouses by amending several existing acts.

*   The Divorce Act is amended to define "spouse" as either of two persons married to each other, removing gender specification.
*   The Marriage (Prohibited Degrees) Act is renamed the Marriage Capacity Act, and its long title is updated to "An Act respecting the capacity to marry."
*   The Marriage Capacity Act is amended to explicitly state that a marriage between two persons is not invalid solely because they are of the same sex.
*   The Modernization of Benefits and Obligations Act is amended to define "marriage" as the lawful union of one man and one woman or two persons of the same sex.

Bill C-208 seeks to amend the Parliament of Canada Act, addressing situations where Members of Parliament change their political affiliation after being elected. The bill aims to ensure that a member's seat is vacated, triggering a by-election, if they switch parties or join a party after being elected as an independent or under a different party.

*   If a member elected under one party's banner or as an independent joins another registered party, their seat is deemed vacant.
*   The leader of the party the member joins is required to notify the Speaker of the House of Commons.
*   Upon notification, the Speaker must issue a warrant to the Chief Electoral Officer, prompting a by-election.
*   If there is no Speaker, the party leader will directly address the warrant to the Chief Electoral Officer.
*   A member's seat is not vacated if they choose to sit as an independent after being elected as a member of a political party.

Bill C-324 is an act to rename the federal electoral district of Welland. Its purpose is to change the district's name to "Niagara South — Centre".

*   The bill amends the representation order of August 25, 2003, under the Electoral Boundaries Readjustment Act.
*   It specifically targets paragraph 97 in the section related to the Province of Ontario.
*   The name "Welland" is replaced with "Niagara South — Centre" in the aforementioned paragraph.
*   The change only affects the name of the electoral district, not its boundaries or representation.

Bill C-270, the Defence of Canada Medal Act (1946-1989), establishes a medal to recognize individuals who served in the defence of Canada during the Cold War period, specifically from June 1, 1946, to November 30, 1989.

Key Points:

*   The medal can be awarded to Canadian citizens or permanent residents with a minimum of three years of cumulative service in the Canadian Forces, certain police services, provincial/municipal emergency organizations, recognized civilian organizations involved in national survival training, or the Canadian Coast Guard during the specified period.
*   The Governor in Council determines the medal's design.
*   Service outside Canada with intergovernmental organizations, at Canadian Forces Station Alert, or on peacekeeping missions is excluded.
*   The medal can be awarded posthumously to the next of kin.
*   Award ceremonies should be held to educate younger Canadians about the historical context.
*   The Ministers of National Defence and Public Safety and Emergency Preparedness nominate qualified members or former members of the Canadian Forces and police forces respectively. Any minister can nominate individuals serving under their administration.
*   The Governor in Council may make regulations regarding eligibility, exclusions, presentation, nomination processes, and definition of next of kin.
*   If regulations aren't made within two years, the Minister must report to Parliament explaining why and provide a schedule for their creation.
*   The Act does not limit the Governor General's powers regarding the medal.

The Perfluorooctane Sulfonate Virtual Elimination Act mandates the addition of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) and its salts to the Virtual Elimination List under the Canadian Environmental Protection Act, 1999.

*   Requires the Minister of the Environment and the Minister of Health to create a regulation adding PFOS and its salts to the Virtual Elimination List within nine months of the Act's enactment.
*   Exempts the Ministers from specifying a level of quantification for PFOS and its salts in the regulation, despite the requirements of subsection 65(2) of the Canadian Environmental Protection Act, 1999.
*   If a level of quantification is specified for PFOS and its salts, the Ministers are not required to make a regulation described in subsection 65(3) of the Canadian Environmental Protection Act, 1999.

Bill C-573 is an act to rename the federal electoral district of Western Arctic. Its purpose is to update the district's name to better reflect its geographical location.

*   The bill changes the name of the electoral district of "Western Arctic" to "Northwest Territories".
*   This amendment updates the representation order declared in force on January 8, 1996, under the Electoral Boundaries Readjustment Act.
*   The change only affects the name of the electoral district and does not alter its boundaries or representation in the House of Commons.

Bill C-243 seeks to revert the name of the Employment Insurance Act to its original name, the Unemployment Insurance Act, and make corresponding changes to related terminology.

*   The long title of the Employment Insurance Act will be replaced with "An Act respecting unemployment insurance in Canada".
*   The short title of the Act will be changed from Employment Insurance Act to Unemployment Insurance Act.
*   All references to "Employment Insurance Act" in other Acts of Parliament will be replaced with "Unemployment Insurance Act".
*   All references to "Employment Insurance Act" in regulations and other instruments will be replaced with "Unemployment Insurance Act".
*   The term "Employment Insurance Account" will be replaced with "Unemployment Insurance Account" throughout the Act.
*   The Department of Human Resources and Skills Development Act is amended to reflect the name change of the Unemployment Insurance Account, ensuring the Auditor General audits this account annually.

Bill C-489, the Inco Limited Acquisition Act, mandates the Government of Canada to disclose specific information related to the 2006 acquisition of Inco Limited by CVRD Canada Inc. The purpose is to increase transparency regarding the commitments made during the acquisition process.

*   Requires the Government of Canada to publish in the Canada Gazette all written undertakings given under the Investment Canada Act regarding the Inco Limited acquisition by CVRD Canada Inc. in 2006.
*   Requires the publication of all demands made by the Minister of Industry related to those undertakings.
*   The publication must occur within 60 days of the Act coming into force.
*   This requirement overrides any conflicting provisions in other Acts of Parliament.

Bill C-23, also known as the Department of Human Resources and Skills Development Act, establishes the Department of Human Resources and Skills Development and defines the roles, responsibilities, and powers of the Minister of Human Resources and Skills Development, the Minister of Labour, and the Canada Employment Insurance Commission. It also outlines rules for the protection and use of personal information collected under departmental programs.

Key changes, impacts, and takeaways:

*   Creates the Department of Human Resources and Skills Development, headed by the Minister of Human Resources and Skills Development.
*   Defines the powers, duties, and functions of the Minister of Human Resources and Skills Development, the Minister of Labour, and the Canada Employment Insurance Commission.
*   Sets out rules for protecting personal information obtained through departmental programs and specifies conditions under which this information can be accessed and shared.
*   Authorizes the Minister to establish programs supporting human resource and skills development projects, including the ability to provide grants and contributions.
*   Allows the Department and the Commission to provide and receive services from the Department of Social Development.
*   Enables the Minister to establish advisory committees.
*   Permits the Minister to enter into agreements with provinces, provincial bodies, financial institutions, and other relevant entities.
*   Grants authority to the Minister to delegate powers, duties, or functions.
*   Allows authorized individuals within the Department to administer oaths and take affidavits.
*   Authorizes the Minister to set fees for services and facilities provided, subject to Treasury Board regulations.
*   Addresses Canada Education Savings Grants.
*   Includes transitional provisions, consequential amendments, coordinating amendments, and repeals the Department of Human Resources Development Act.

This bill amends the National Defence Act. Its purpose is to modify provisions related to the powers and duties of the Governor in Council regarding the making of regulations.

*   Removes paragraph (c) from Section 98 of the Act.
*   Modifies paragraph (a) by adding "or" at the end.
*   Removes "or" at the end of paragraph (b).
*   These changes relate to the regulation-making authority of the Governor in Council concerning matters outlined in Section 98.

Bill C-429 amends the Department of Public Works and Government Services Act to prioritize the use of wood in construction projects.

*   Requires the Minister of Public Works and Government Services to favor designs that incorporate wood in the construction, maintenance, or repair of public works, federal immovables, and federal real property before soliciting bids.
*   Mandates consideration of both cost and greenhouse gas emissions when prioritizing the use of wood.
*   The amendment applies despite existing provisions in the Department of Public Works and Government Services Act.

Bill C-243 seeks to revert the name of the Employment Insurance Act to its original name, the Unemployment Insurance Act, and make corresponding changes to related terminology.

*   The long title of the Employment Insurance Act will be replaced with "An Act respecting unemployment insurance in Canada".
*   The short title of the Act will be changed from Employment Insurance Act to Unemployment Insurance Act.
*   All references to "Employment Insurance Act" in other Acts of Parliament will be replaced with "Unemployment Insurance Act".
*   All references to "Employment Insurance Act" in regulations and other instruments will be replaced with "Unemployment Insurance Act".
*   The term "Employment Insurance Account" will be replaced with "Unemployment Insurance Account" throughout the Act.
*   The Department of Human Resources and Skills Development Act is amended to reflect the name change of the Unemployment Insurance Account, ensuring the Auditor General audits this account annually.

Bill C-409, also known as the Canada-Portugal Day Act, designates June 10th of every year as "Canada-Portugal Day." The purpose is to recognize and express gratitude for the contributions of the Portuguese-Canadian community to Canadian society.

*   Establishes June 10th as Canada-Portugal Day annually across Canada.
*   Acknowledges the history and contributions of Portuguese-Canadians.
*   Specifies that Canada-Portugal Day is not a legal holiday or a non-juridical day.
*   Does not create any new legal obligations or rights.
*   Symbolically recognizes the cultural roots of persons of Portuguese origin who celebrate the life of Luis de Camões.

Bill S-210 aims to repeal the short title of An Act to amend the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act, the Civil Marriage Act and the Criminal Code and to make consequential amendments to other Acts.

*   The bill specifically repeals section 1 of the Act, which is chapter 29 of the Statutes of Canada, 2015.
*   The repealed section provided the short title "Zero Tolerance for Barbaric Cultural Practices Act".
*   The effect of this bill is to remove the aforementioned short title from the Act.

Bill C-395 is an act to change the name of the federal electoral district of Welland. Its purpose is to rename the riding to better reflect the geographical area it represents.

*   The bill amends the 2003 Representation Order for Ontario.
*   The electoral district name "Welland" is replaced with "Niagara Centre — South".
*   This change only affects the name of the electoral district and does not alter its boundaries or representation.

Bill C-557 amends the Telefilm Canada Act to specifically address Quebec audio-visual productions and ensure representation for Quebec within the Telefilm Canada Corporation.

*   Mandates that a minimum of two members of the Telefilm Canada Corporation must reside in Quebec.
*   Directs the Corporation to foster and promote the development of Quebec's audio-visual industry, recognizing its distinctiveness.
*   Allows the Corporation to invest in, make loans to, and provide awards for Quebec audio-visual productions, similar to Canadian audio-visual productions.
*   Defines "Quebec audio-visual production" based on significant Quebec creative, artistic, and technical content, as well as copyright ownership by Quebec residents or businesses, or through co-production agreements with other countries involving Quebec.

Bill C-654 amends the Parliament of Canada Act to restrict the use of omnibus bills in the Senate and the House of Commons. Its purpose is to limit the scope of bills that can be adopted.

*   It prohibits the adoption of bills that amend, enact, or repeal more than one Act, or a combination of these actions, unless the bill relates to a single subject matter or clearly interrelated subject matters implementing a single broad policy.
*   It provides exceptions for Miscellaneous Statute Law Amendment Acts.
*   It provides exceptions for bills implementing a federal budget, provided all substantive provisions are primarily financial.

Bill S-10, also known as the Prohibiting Cluster Munitions Act, implements Canada’s commitments under the Convention on Cluster Munitions by establishing prohibitions and offences related to cluster munitions, explosive submunitions, and explosive bomblets.

Key changes, impacts, and takeaways:
*   Prohibits the use, development, making, acquisition, and possession of cluster munitions, explosive submunitions, and explosive bomblets.
*   Prohibits the import, export, and movement of cluster munitions, explosive submunitions, and explosive bomblets between foreign states with the intent to transfer ownership and control.
*   Defines key terms such as "cluster munition," "explosive submunition," and "explosive bomblet," outlining specific exclusions for certain types of munitions.
*   Allows designated ministers to grant exemptions for activities related to the development of and training in detection, clearance, destruction techniques, and counter-measures related to cluster munitions.
*   Includes exceptions for the destruction of cluster munitions, explosive submunitions, and explosive bomblets on behalf of the Canadian Forces or the Department of National Defence, and for destruction not on behalf of those entities.
*   Provides exceptions for military cooperation or combined military operations with states not party to the Convention, under specific conditions and limitations.
*   Includes an exception for peace officers and others acting in the course of their duties.
*   Specifies that the Act is binding on Her Majesty in right of Canada or a province.

Bill C-370 proposes amendments to the Employment Insurance Act concerning the length of the benefit period. The aim is to increase the duration of benefits available to claimants.

*   A week where a claimant worked at least 15 hours will be counted as a full week of insurable employment.
*   The total number of hours worked by a claimant during the qualifying period will be divided by 30, and this number will represent the minimum number of weeks of insurable employment, potentially increasing the benefit period.
*   The maximum length of a benefit period remains 52 weeks, aligning with the existing framework of the Act.

Bill C-507, also known as the Pay Equity Task Force Recommendations Act, mandates the Government of Canada to implement the recommendations outlined in the Pay Equity Task Force's final report of May 5, 2004.

*   Requires the Government of Canada to take immediate action to implement the recommendations of the Pay Equity Task Force.
*   Focuses on improving existing legislation and policies related to pay equity.
*   Obliges the Minister of Labour to prepare annual reports for five fiscal years, starting April 1, 2008, detailing the government's progress in fulfilling its obligations.
*   These reports must be presented to both Houses of Parliament within 90 days of the fiscal year's end.

This bill, called the Recognition of Certain Métis Governments in Alberta, Ontario and Saskatchewan and Métis Self-Government Act, aims to formally recognize Métis governments and advance their right to self-determination. It also establishes a framework for implementing treaties between these governments and the Canadian government.

Key changes, impacts, and takeaways:

*   **Recognition of Métis Governments:** Formally recognizes specific Métis governments as Indigenous governing bodies with the authority to act on behalf of their Métis collectivities, affirming their right to self-determination.
*   **Framework for Treaties:** Establishes a process for tabling treaties in Parliament, giving them legal force, and ensuring they prevail over conflicting federal laws.
*   **Supplementary Self-Government Agreements:** Sets out a similar process for supplementary agreements, including tabling in Parliament and giving them the force of law.
*   **Tax Treatment Agreements:** Approves tax treatment agreements, giving them legal force, but clarifies they are not treaties or land claims agreements under the Constitution.
*   **Judicial Notice and Publication:** Mandates judicial notice of treaties, supplementary agreements, tax agreements, and Métis government laws, and requires their publication.
*   **Notice Requirements:** Requires notice to the Attorney General of Canada and relevant Métis governments when the interpretation, validity, or applicability of the Act, a treaty, an agreement, or a Métis government law is challenged in legal proceedings.
*   **Exemption from Statutory Instruments Act:** Exempts Métis government laws from the *Statutory Instruments Act*.

Bill C-266, also known as the Pope John Paul II Day Act, establishes a day to commemorate Pope John Paul II's life and legacy. It aims to recognize his contributions to the Roman Catholic Church, international understanding, and peace.

*   Designates April 2nd of each year as "Pope John Paul II Day" throughout Canada.
*   Specifies that Pope John Paul II Day is not a legal holiday or non-juridical day.
*   Acknowledges Pope John Paul II's role in promoting international understanding and peace.
*   Recognizes his connection to young people through the establishment of World Youth Day.
*   Highlights his visits to Canada and his contribution to ending communism in Eastern Europe.

Bill C-468 amends the Aeronautics Act, focusing on provincial authority regarding aerodrome location and land use near airports. It aims to increase provincial input in decisions concerning aerodromes and adjacent land development.

*   Requires Governor in Council to obtain provincial approval for regulations about the location of aerodromes.
*   Mandates the relevant minister to attempt to reach an agreement with the provincial government before enacting zoning regulations preventing land use or development near airports or airport sites.
*   Allows the Governor in Council to make zoning regulations without provincial agreement only if the minister deems it immediately necessary to prevent land use or development that contravenes the Act.

Bill C-449, also known as the National Local Food Day Act, aims to designate a specific day to celebrate and promote locally sourced food across Canada.

*   Establishes the Friday before Thanksgiving Day as "National Local Food Day" annually.
*   Aims to strengthen the connection between consumers and Canadian food producers.
*   Seeks to support local farmers and contribute to a sustainable Canadian agricultural industry.
*   Clarifies that National Local Food Day is not a legal holiday or non-juridical day.

Bill C-78 amends the Divorce Act, the Family Orders and Agreements Enforcement Assistance Act, and the Garnishment, Attachment and Pension Diversion Act, and makes consequential amendments to another Act. Its purpose is to modernize family law, prioritize the best interests of the child, and address family violence.

Key Changes, Impacts, and Takeaways:

*   Replaces "custody" and "access" with parenting terminology like "parenting orders" and "parenting time."
*   Establishes a non-exhaustive list of criteria for determining the best interests of the child, emphasizing safety and well-being.
*   Creates a duty for parties and legal advisors to encourage family dispute resolution processes like mediation.
*   Introduces measures to assist courts in addressing family violence, including defining "family violence" and outlining its impact.
*   Establishes a framework for the relocation of a child, considering its impact on relationships with parents and other individuals.
*   Simplifies processes related to family support obligations.
*   Amends the Family Orders and Agreements Enforcement Assistance Act to allow the release of information to help obtain and vary support provisions, expand information release to provincial family justice entities, permit garnishment of federal monies for family law expenses, and extend the binding period of garnishee summonses.
*   Implements the Hague Convention on Jurisdiction, Applicable Law, Recognition, Enforcement and Co-operation in Respect of Parental Responsibility and Measures for the Protection of Children, and the Hague Convention on the International Recovery of Child Support and Other Forms of Family Maintenance.
*   Amends the Garnishment, Attachment and Pension Diversion Act to give priority to family support obligations and simplify processes.
*   Includes transitional provisions and consequential amendments to the Criminal Code.

Bill C-377 is an act to change the name of the federal electoral district of Châteauguay—Lacolle. The purpose of the bill is to rename the riding.

*   The bill amends the representation order of October 1, 2013, under the Electoral Boundaries Readjustment Act.
*   The electoral district's name is changed from "Châteauguay—Lacolle" to "Châteauguay—Les Jardins-de-Napierville."
*   The change only affects the name of the electoral district, with no other changes to its boundaries or representation.

Bill S-211, the International Mother Language Day Act, aims to formally recognize the importance of linguistic and cultural diversity in Canada. It seeks to establish a designated day to celebrate mother languages.

*   Designates February 21st of each year as "International Mother Language Day" throughout Canada.
*   Clarifies that International Mother Language Day is not a legal holiday or a non-juridical day.
*   Acknowledges Canada's official languages, the numerous Aboriginal languages spoken, and the multitude of languages spoken by Canadians that enrich the country.
*   Recognizes the United Nations' proclamation of International Mother Language Day and its call for the preservation and protection of all languages.
*   Highlights that some Canadian provinces and cities already recognize International Mother Language Day.

The Food Day in Canada Act designates a specific day to celebrate Canadian food.

*   The bill establishes "Food Day in Canada" to be observed annually.
*   Food Day in Canada will be held on the Saturday before the first Monday in August each year.
*   The Act applies across Canada.
*   The purpose is to recognize and celebrate Canadian food and agricultural products.

The First Nations Clean Water Act aims to ensure First Nations have access to clean, safe drinking water and effective wastewater treatment by affirming their right to self-government and establishing principles for water service decision-making.

Key changes, impacts, and takeaways:

*   **Affirms First Nations Rights:** Upholds the rights of First Nations peoples recognized in the Constitution Act, 1982, and recognizes access to clean and safe drinking water as a human right.
*   **Purpose:** Ensures reliable access to safe drinking water and effective wastewater treatment, affirms First Nations' self-government over water-related matters, and aligns laws with the Constitution and the UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples.
*   **Principles:** Emphasizes reliable access to water services, substantive equality, and adherence to the UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples in decision-making.
*   **Jurisdiction:** Affirms First Nations' jurisdiction over water, source water, drinking water, wastewater, and related infrastructure on their lands, including legislative authority and the power to administer and enforce First Nation laws.
*   **Application of Charter:** The Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms applies to First Nation governing bodies when exercising jurisdiction related to this Act.
*   **Prevalence of Laws:** Specific federal laws related to fisheries, navigation, environmental protection, and species at risk prevail over First Nation laws in cases of conflict. First Nation laws generally prevail over other federal laws, except for specific sections of this Act and certain federal acts like the Access to Information Act.
*   **Standards:** Drinking water quality on First Nation lands must meet Canadian Drinking Water Quality Guidelines or provincial/territorial standards, as chosen by the First Nation, and wastewater effluent must meet Wastewater Systems Effluent Regulations standards or provincial/territorial standards, also by First Nation choice.
*   **Modern Treaties:** Modern treaties and self-government agreements prevail over this Act and its regulations in cases of inconsistency, with the option to amend these agreements to resolve conflicts.

Bill C-281 amends the Canada Shipping Act, 2001, to broaden eligibility requirements for certificates of competency.

*   The bill allows foreign nationals who have obtained a diploma from a designated Canadian maritime institution to be eligible for a certificate of competency.
*   It modifies the definition of "qualified person" under the Act to include these foreign nationals.
*   The amendment also empowers the Minister to designate Canadian maritime institutions whose diplomas will qualify foreign nationals for certificates of competency.
*   Only Canadian citizens, permanent residents, and now, certain foreign nationals are permitted to hold a certificate of competency.

Bill C-458 amends the Canada Post Corporation Act to reduce postage rates for library materials.

*   Defines "library materials" to include books, magazines, records, CDs, CD-ROMs, audio cassettes, video cassettes, DVDs, and other audio-visual materials.
*   Allows for prescribing a reduced postage rate for library materials.
*   Enables regulations for compensating the Canada Post Corporation for the reduced postage rate for library materials.
*   Permits the Canada Post Corporation, with approval from the Governor in Council, to enter into an agreement with the Department of Canadian Heritage to continue the Library Book Rate.

Bill S-211, also known as the Modern Slavery Act, aims to combat forced and child labor by imposing reporting obligations on certain entities and amending the Customs Tariff to prohibit the import of goods produced using such labor.

Key changes, impacts, and takeaways:

*   **Reporting Obligations:** Entities involved in the production, sale, or import of goods must submit an annual report to the Minister of Public Safety and Emergency Preparedness, outlining steps taken to prevent and reduce the risk of forced or child labor in their supply chains.
*   **Report Content:** The report must include information on the entity’s structure, policies, risk assessment activities, remediation measures, and employee training related to forced and child labor.
*   **Public Accessibility:** Entities must make their reports publicly available, including posting them on their websites.
*   **Enforcement Powers:** Designated persons have the authority to enter premises, examine documents and data, and take other measures to verify compliance with the Act.
*   **Corrective Measures:** The Minister can order entities to take corrective measures if they are not in compliance with reporting requirements.
*   **Offences and Penalties:** Failure to comply with reporting obligations, obstruction of designated persons, and providing false information are offenses punishable by fines up to $250,000.
*   **Customs Tariff Amendment:** The bill amends the Customs Tariff to prohibit the importation of goods manufactured or produced, in whole or in part, by forced labor or child labor.
*   **Definition of Entities:** The Act applies to corporations, trusts, partnerships, or other organizations listed on a Canadian stock exchange or meeting specific criteria related to assets, revenue, or employee numbers.

Georgina's Law focuses on preventing and addressing intimate partner violence in Canada. It mandates national action led by the Minister for Women and Gender Equality and Youth.

*   The Minister must lead national action to prevent and address intimate partner violence.
*   The Minister is required to engage annually with federal and provincial ministers, Indigenous partners, victims, survivors, and stakeholders. These engagements will assess current programs, partnerships, costs, and legal implications related to preventing intimate partner violence.
*   These engagements must begin within one year of the bill receiving royal assent.
*   The Minister must prepare and table a report in Parliament every two years on the progress of actions taken, and publish the report on the departmental website within 30 days of tabling.
*   The definition of "intimate partner" includes current or former spouses, common-law partners, and dating partners.

This bill amends the Copyright Act regarding Crown copyright. It aims to remove copyright protection for works prepared or published by or under the direction or control of His Majesty or any government department.

*   Removes copyright protection for works created or published by the government.
*   Specifies that existing copyrights held by the Crown cease to exist when the amendment comes into force.
*   Preserves any existing rights or privileges of the Crown, aside from copyright.

The Framework on the Access to and Use of Cash Act aims to ensure continued access to and use of cash in Canada. It mandates the Minister of Finance to develop and implement a framework for this purpose and includes amendments to the Currency Act and the Bank of Canada Act.

*   Requires the Minister of Finance to conduct a comprehensive review of existing legislation, regulations, and policies related to cash availability within financial institutions within 90 days of the Act coming into force.
*   Directs the Minister to develop a framework within 18 months to ensure continued access to cash, including measures to:
    *   Ensure infrastructure for cash withdrawals and deposits across Canada.
    *   Protect the ability to use cash despite digital currencies.
    *   Incentivize businesses to accept cash payments.
    *   Remove barriers to cash donations for non-profits.
    *   Ensure the long-term viability of Canada’s cash infrastructure.
*   Mandates the Minister to table a report in Parliament within 18 months, outlining the framework and related recommendations, and to publish the report on the Department of Finance's website within 10 days of tabling.
*   Requires the Minister to undertake a review of the implementation, effectiveness, and social and economic impacts of the framework within three years of the report being tabled, and to table a report of the review in Parliament.
*   Repeals subsection 9(1) of the Currency Act and prohibits the Bank of Canada from issuing a central bank digital currency by amending the Bank of Canada Act.

The Chemical Weapons Convention Implementation Act updates the definition of "Convention" and removes obsolete sections.

*   The definition of "Convention" is updated to include amendments made to the original treaty signed in 1993.
*   Subsection 2(3) of the Act, the content of which is not specified in the provided text, is repealed.
*   The schedule to the Act is repealed, the contents of which are not specified in the provided text.

The National Security Review of Investments Modernization Act amends the Investment Canada Act to modernize the review process for investments by non-Canadians, particularly concerning national security.

*   Expands notification requirements to include investments where a non-Canadian could gain access to material non-public technical information or assets in prescribed business activities.
*   Introduces a pre-implementation notification requirement for certain investments, prohibiting implementation until specified review periods expire.
*   Modifies the timelines for the Director to issue a notice for review, providing greater certainty for investors.
*   Updates the definitions of "WTO investor" and "trade agreement investor" by changing "two thirds" to "at least two thirds" for clarity regarding board members, general partners, and trustees.
*   Clarifies that investments subject to notification under Part III, excluding those related to access to sensitive information, are reviewable under certain conditions.
*   Revises the period for filing an application for review in specific investment scenarios.
*   Amends the French version of the Act regarding the extension of the period during which the Minister may send a notice, aligning it with existing provisions.
